criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the problem formulation of prompt tuning and the standard prompt embedding setup.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the core ULPT methodology: decomposing the prompt embedding matrix into low-dimensional embeddings and a frozen random projection matrix.
The agent has read Equation 3 to understand how each entry in the up-projected embedding matrix is computed using the low-dimensional embeddings, frozen projection, shift, and scale vectors.
The plan includes implementing the prompt tuning framework with learnable prefix embeddings prepended to the input as described in Equation 1.
The plan includes the ULPT decomposition: learning only r-dimensional prompt embeddings Z while keeping the projection matrix P frozen and randomly initialized.
The plan includes the learnable shift embedding b ∈ R^d and scale embedding s ∈ R^d shared across prompt token positions.
The plan specifies that only the random seed is stored to reconstruct the frozen projection matrix P̃ when an LLM is loaded.
The code for the standard prompt tuning input structure has been implemented as Equation 1: learnable prompt embeddings (e_1, ..., e_n) followed by tokenized text embeddings (x_1, ..., x_l).
The code for the conditional log-likelihood objective has been implemented as Equation 2: arg max_E Σ_{(x,y)∈D} log P(y|E,x).

The code for the ULPT up-projected embedding has been implemented as Equation 3: ê_{ij} = (Σ_{k=1}^{r} z_{ik} p̃_{kj}) s_j + b_j, where z_{ik} and p̃_{kj} are entries in Z and P̃, and s_j, b_j are entries in the scale and shift vectors.
The code initializes the frozen random projection matrix P̃ ∈ R^{r×d} from a standard normal distribution and keeps it frozen (not updated) during training.
The code stores only a single random seed to reconstruct the frozen projection matrix P̃, rather than storing the full matrix.
The total number of trainable parameters in ULPT is nr + 2d (n prompt tokens × rank r, plus two d-dimensional shift and scale vectors), not including the frozen projection.
The learnable shift embedding b and scale embedding s are shared across all prompt token positions but independent per task.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the language understanding experiment setup, including the 21 NLP tasks grouped into 4 categories.
The agent has read Appendix A.2 to check the training hyperparameters for language understanding experiments.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmark results, including all baseline comparisons.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing all 21 NLP tasks: GLUE (MNLI, QQP, QNLI, SST-2, STS-B, MRPC, RTE, CoLA), SuperGLUE (MultiRC, BoolQ, WiC, WSC, CB), MRQA (NQ, HotpotQA, SearchQA, NewsQA), and Others (WinoGrande, Yelp-2, SciTail, PAWS-Wiki).
The plan specifies using the T5-base model (d=768) as the backbone for language understanding experiments.
The plan specifies evaluating ULPT with four rank configurations: r=2, r=16, r=64, and r=256, with n=100 prompt tokens.
The plan includes implementing the baselines: vanilla prompt tuning (PT), DPT (with r=10 and r=64), and DePT.
The plan specifies the training hyperparameters: batch size 16, learning rate 6e-4, AdamW optimizer, linear schedule with 500-step warm-up, and training for up to 100,000 steps.
The plan specifies the maximum sequence lengths: 256 for most tasks, 348 for SuperGLUE-MultiRC, and 512 for MRQA tasks.
The code for loading and preprocessing the 8 GLUE benchmark datasets (MNLI, QQP, QNLI, SST-2, STS-B, MRPC, RTE, CoLA) has been implemented.
The code for loading and preprocessing the 5 SuperGLUE benchmark datasets (MultiRC, BoolQ, WiC, WSC, CB) has been implemented.
The code for loading and preprocessing the 4 MRQA datasets (Natural Questions, HotpotQA, SearchQA, NewsQA) has been implemented.
The code for loading and preprocessing the 4 other classification tasks (WinoGrande, Yelp-2, SciTail, PAWS-Wiki) has been implemented.
The code preprocesses classification and multiple-choice task labels into single-token labels (e.g., 0, 1, 2, ...) as described in Appendix A.1.
The code implements ULPT training with T5-base using AdamW optimizer, batch size 16, learning rate 6e-4, linear schedule with 500-step warm-up, and up to 100,000 training steps.
The code evaluates performance every 1,000 steps and selects the best checkpoint based on the validation set.
The code reports Pearson correlation for STS-B, F1 for MultiRC and MRQA tasks, and accuracy for all other classification tasks.
The main ULPT experiments on GLUE and SuperGLUE with T5-base have been executed successfully without errors for all four rank configurations (r=2, 16, 64, 256).
The ULPT experiments on MRQA and other classification tasks with T5-base have been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: ULPT at r=2 achieves at least 97% of vanilla PT performance on GLUE while using only 1.7K parameters (98% parameter reduction vs PT's 76.8K).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: ULPT at r=64 (7.9K params) outperforms vanilla PT (76.8K params) on average GLUE score, achieving approximately 86.0 vs 84.9.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: ULPT at r=64 outperforms DPT at r=64 (55.6K params) on GLUE average despite using 7x fewer parameters.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: ULPT on MRQA tasks shows improvement with increasing rank, with ULPT (r=256) achieving competitive performance close to DePT.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the reasoning task experiments using decoder-only Llama 3.2 models.
The agent has read Appendix A.3 to check the implementation and training details for reasoning tasks.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the efficiency and performance results on GSM8K and MBPP tasks.
The plan includes evaluating ULPT on Llama 3.2 models at 1B (d=2048) and 3B (d=3072) scales on GSM8K and MBPP tasks.
The plan includes implementing baselines for reasoning tasks: 4-shot ICL, LoRA (r=1,4,8), VeRA (r=1,4,8), FourierFT (n=128,512,1024), IA3, and vanilla PT.
The plan specifies the reasoning task training hyperparameters: batch size 4, 3 epochs for GSM8K, 10 epochs for MBPP, learning rate 1e-3 for LoRA/VeRA, 1e-2 for ULPT/PT/FourierFT.
The plan specifies using 10 learnable tokens for vanilla PT and ULPT on Llama models, with rank configurations r=2, 64, 256.
The code for ULPT on Llama 3.2 (1B and 3B) has been implemented and correctly handles the larger hidden dimensionalities (d=2048 and d=3072).
The code for LoRA, VeRA, FourierFT, IA3, and vanilla PT baselines on Llama 3.2 models has been implemented.
The code applies greedy decoding with maximum generation length of 1024 tokens at inference time for reasoning tasks.
The code for ULPT and all baselines on Llama 3.2 models has been executed successfully without errors on both GSM8K and MBPP tasks.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ULPT at r=64 achieves the best performance on Llama 1B (GSM8K: ~42.4, MBPP: ~28.7, avg ~35.6) using only 4.7K parameters.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ULPT uses significantly less VRAM than LoRA/VeRA/FourierFT while achieving higher accuracy, confirming memory efficiency.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ULPT has faster training runtime (seconds/1K steps) compared to FourierFT, IA3, and LoRA at the same parameter scale.
The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the dimension-length trade-off analysis comparing ULPT with fixed token count (n=100, varying rank) vs vanilla PT with fixed rank (varying token count).
The agent has read Figure 3 to understand that ULPT with longer low-dimensional prompts consistently outperforms vanilla PT with fewer high-dimensional tokens under controlled parameter budgets.
The agent has read Figure 5 to understand the ablation study on shift and scale embeddings: no alignment, shift only, and both shift and scale.

The plan includes the dimension-length trade-off experiment: fixing n=100 for ULPT (varying rank 2 to 256) and varying token count (2 to 50) for vanilla PT, on MNLI and Natural Questions across T5-small, T5-base, and T5-large.
The plan includes the prompt length sensitivity experiment (Figure 4): varying n from 10 to 100 for both ULPT and vanilla PT.
The plan includes the ablation study on shift and scale embeddings using SST-2 with T5-base (n=100): three conditions — no alignment, learnable shift only (w/ b), both shift and scale (w/ b and s).
The plan includes the comparison between tuning Z (frozen P̃, our ULPT) and tuning P (frozen Z) under controlled parameter budgets on MNLI and Natural Questions.

The code for the dimension-length trade-off experiment is implemented: ULPT with fixed n=100 and varying rank (2, 16, 64, 256) vs vanilla PT with varying token count (2 to 50 tokens) across T5-small (d=512), T5-base (d=768), and T5-large (d=1024).
The code for the prompt length sensitivity experiment is implemented: varying n from 10 to 100 for both ULPT and vanilla PT, with different rank configurations.

The code for the ablation study on shift and scale embeddings is implemented with three conditions: (1) no shift/scale (plain frozen projection), (2) learnable shift b only, (3) both shift b and scale s.
The code for the comparison between tuning Z (our ULPT) vs tuning P under controlled parameter budgets is implemented on MNLI and Natural Questions with T5-base.
The dimension-length trade-off experiment has been executed successfully without errors across three T5 model sizes on MNLI and Natural Questions.
The ablation study on shift and scale embeddings has been executed successfully without errors, producing training loss curves and evaluation accuracy curves.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: ULPT with lower-dimensional embeddings and more tokens (solid lines) consistently outperforms vanilla PT with fewer high-dimensional tokens (dashed lines) under the same parameter budget across all three model sizes.
The execution outcomes match Figure 4: ULPT performance improves with longer prompts (n=10 to 100), and ULPT with appropriate rank consistently outperforms vanilla PT across all prompt lengths.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: removing both shift and scale leads to high training loss (training fails to converge); adding shift b substantially improves optimization; adding both shift b and scale s further improves training loss and accuracy.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: tuning P (frozen Z) is infeasible at 1.7K parameters, and with equivalent parameter budgets, tuning Z (our ULPT) achieves slightly better performance than tuning P.
The agent has read Appendix B.3 to understand the Bloomz family analysis: comparing ULPT vs vanilla PT across three model sizes (560M, 1.7B, 3B) on SST-2, HotpotQA, and WinoGrande.
The agent has read Appendix B.4 to check the inference overhead analysis comparing ULPT up-projection time vs vanilla PT and end-to-end decoding speed.
The plan includes the Bloomz family analysis: evaluating ULPT on Bloomz (560M, 1.7B, 3B) on SST-2, HotpotQA, and WinoGrande under controlled parameter budgets.
The plan specifies Bloomz training details: up to 30K steps, batch size 4, same learning rates as the main experiment.
The plan includes the inference overhead analysis: measuring ULPT up-projection runtime (rank 2, 64, 256) vs vanilla PT loading, and end-to-end decoding speed comparison.
The code for evaluating ULPT on Bloomz (560M, 1.7B, 3B) with hidden dimensions (1024, 2048, 2560) on SST-2, HotpotQA, and WinoGrande has been implemented.
The code for measuring ULPT up-projection runtime at inference time (rank 2, 64, 256) compared to vanilla PT embedding loading has been implemented.
The Bloomz family experiments have been executed successfully without errors across all three model sizes and three tasks.
The execution outcomes match Table 6: ULPT consistently outperforms vanilla PT under controlled parameter budgets across Bloomz-560M, 1.7B, and 3B models.
The execution outcomes match Table 7: ULPT up-projection overhead is negligible compared to decoding time (e.g., ULPT r=2 takes ~0.56ms for Llama 1B vs decoding ~1481ms).
The execution outcomes match Table 8: ULPT does not meaningfully degrade decoding speed (tokens/second) compared to no adaptation baseline.
The agent has read Figure 6 to understand the pairwise cosine similarity analysis of shift and scale embeddings across different rank configurations.
The plan includes analyzing pairwise cosine similarities of shift (b) and scale (s) vectors learned with different rank configurations.
The code for computing pairwise cosine similarities between shift embeddings and between scale embeddings learned with different ranks (r=2, 16, 64, 256) has been implemented.

The execution outcomes match Figure 6: shift embeddings show consistently high cosine similarity across different ranks (indicating a stable alignment role), while scale embeddings show near-zero cosine similarity (indicating dependence on the random projection).
The agent has read Appendix B.2 to check additional results on MRQA and other classification tasks, including mean and standard deviation over 3 runs with different seeds.

ULPT experiments on MRQA and other classification tasks are run three times with different random seeds to report mean and standard deviation, assessing sensitivity to random projection initialization.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: ULPT on MRQA and Other tasks shows monotonic improvement as rank increases from r=2 to r=256, with ULPT (r=256) achieving competitive performance relative to DePT.
